---
title: "Introduction to R Workshop"
subtitle: "Getting Started, Data Visualization, and Regression"
author: "Muji Chughtai"
date: today
format:
  beamer:
    theme: default
    aspectratio: 169
    slide-level: 2
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    include-in-header: beamer-header.tex
    keep-tex: false
    incremental: true
---

# Getting Started

## Introduction to R and RStudio

- R is a free software environment for statistical computing and graphics that can compile and run on a variety of platforms.

  - To download R, go to <https://cran.r-project.org>.

- For a more user-friendly interface, RStudio is a popular integrated development environment (IDE) for R.

- RStudio also allows for powerful document creation using Markdown, which can generate reports, presentations, and dashboards (this presentation was made using a Quarto Markdown in RStudio).

  - To download RStudio, go to <https://www.rstudio.com/products/rstudio/download>.

## Getting Familiar with the RStudio Interface

There are 4 main panels in the RStudio interface:

1. **Source Panel (Top Left)**: This is where you can write and edit your R scripts and markdown files. Toggling between tabs allows you to manage multiple files at once.

2. **Console Panel (Top Right)**: This is where you can directly type and execute R commands. When you run code from the Source panel, the output will also appear here.

3. **Environment/History Panel (Bottom Left)**: The Environment tab shows the objects (variables, data frames, functions, etc.) currently in your R session.

4. **Files/Plots/Packages/Help/Viewer Panel (Bottom Right)**: This panel has multiple tabs for managing files, viewing plots, installing and loading packages, accessing R help documentation, and displaying web content.

## Using Markdown


$$E = mc^2$$

::: block
## Theorem

For all real numbers $x$ and $y$, we have $(x+y)^2 = x^2 + 2xy + y^2$
:::

## Academic Environments

Use `::: {.block}`, `::: {.alertblock}`, `::: {.exampleblock}` for Pandoc-native Beamer blocks. Use raw LaTeX (```` ```{=latex} ````) for the custom orange/gray environments.

```{=latex}
\begin{definitionblock}
A \textbf{group} $(G, \cdot)$ is a set with a binary operation satisfying
closure, associativity, identity, and invertibility.
\end{definitionblock}

\begin{proofblock}
By hypothesis every element of $H \leq G$ satisfies $g^n = e$ for some $n$.
Therefore $H$ is generated by $g^k$ for the smallest such $k$.
\end{proofblock}
```

::: alertblock
## Important

This theorem requires the axiom of choice for infinite collections.
:::

## More Environments

::: exampleblock
## Example

The integers $(\mathbb{Z}, +)$ form an abelian group under addition.
:::

```{=latex}
\begin{lemmablock}
Every subgroup of a cyclic group is cyclic.
\end{lemmablock}

\begin{corollaryblock}
If $|G| = p$ is prime then $G \cong \mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}$.
\end{corollaryblock}
```

## Lists and Structure

- First point with important information
- Second point to demonstrate spacing
- Third point showing the style

1.  Numbered lists also work well
2.  With proper spacing
3.  And clean formatting

## Two Column Layout

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Left Column**

- Item one
- Item two
- Item three
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Right Column**

Content on the right side with matching styling
:::
:::::

## Code Example

```{r, echo = TRUE}
# R code with syntax highlighting
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_minimal()
```
